Neural rendering is a technique that involves generating images or scenes using neural networks. The community of researchers or practitioners working on neural rendering is mentioned in the context.
Neural rendering has potential applications in various fields. The context mentions the potential for more promising applications of neural rendering that are still being explored.
Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) represent scenes using MLPs and are used for rendering novel views. NeRF is a key concept in the field of neural rendering mentioned in the context.
NeRF scenes are represented using simple Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLPs). MLPs play a crucial role in the storage and querying of radiance fields for rendering in NeRF.

NeRF-based methods like Neural Radiance Fields have been subject to enhancement efforts. The context mentions efforts to extend NeRF to unbounded scenes, handle moving objects, and reconstruct scenes from pictures in the wild.

Mip-NeRF 360 is mentioned as a method that can handle unbounded scenes. However, it faces challenges with reflective surfaces, impacting the multi-view consistency that is crucial for methods like NeRF.
Mip-NeRF 360 is highlighted for its ability to generate virtual images of scenes. However, these virtual images may violate multi-view consistency, affecting the quality of the rendered scenes.
A new method (possibly introduced in the context) is mentioned as an improvement over conventional NeRF-like methods. The method aims to help NeRF-like methods learn virtual images efficiently.
NeRF methods rely on the multi-view consistency property of scenes. The presence of mirrors in scenes poses a challenge as it disrupts the multi-view consistency assumed by NeRF methods.

The mirror surface and its reflected virtual image have no consistency between the front and back views. This lack of consistency makes it necessary to manually label the reflective surfaces to avoid sub-optimal convergences.
The proposed multi-space NeRF-based method aims to handle mirror-like objects in 360-degree high-fidelity rendering of scenes automatically without the need for manual labeling.
The Euclidean scene space is treated as composed of multiple virtual sub-spaces in the proposed method. The composition of these sub-spaces changes depending on the location and view direction.
The approach using multi-space decomposition successfully handles complex reflections and refractions where multi-view consistency is heavily violated in the Euclidean real space.
The multi-space approach serves as a general enhancement to the NeRF-based backbone, enabling most NeRF-like methods to model complex reflection and refraction.
The 360-degree rendering is specifically dedicated to scenes containing mirror-like objects, as mentioned in the context.
RFFR dataset only has forward-facing scenes as opposed to scenes with mirror-like objects.
The Shiny dataset only includes scenes with minor viewpoint changes, limiting its ability to exhibit view-dependent effects in large angles.

Both reconstruction and view synthesis are key concepts related to generating high-quality images or scenes from multiple viewpoints.
Reconstruction is dependent on different viewpoints for generating a complete scene or image.
Reconstruction may involve handling objects with high-specular materials, which can introduce challenges in image synthesis.
View synthesis often involves creating images or scenes that provide a full 360-degree view for the viewer.
360-degree views typically include forward-facing perspectives for a comprehensive viewing experience.
In 360-degree views, objects with non-Lambertian surfaces may need to be accurately represented for realistic scene rendering.
Non-Lambertian objects with high-specular surfaces may require special handling in scene reconstruction or view synthesis.
View synthesis techniques may need to consider rendering of scenes with glossy materials to achieve realistic visual effects.
